terms.definition_en_simple: A document or object made during the time being studied, by someone who was there.
A book or article written later about a past event, like a textbook.
A written or printed source, such as a letter, law, or speech.
How the author sees an event because of who they are.
Unfairly favoring one side; an opinion that leans one way.
The reason the author made the document.
The people a document was made for.
What was happening at the time and place a source was created.
Why something happened.
What happened because of a cause.
An event that causes a big change in history.
Facts and details that prove a claim.
A statement you try to prove with evidence.
To explain how things are alike.
To explain how things are different.
A period of 100 years.
A long period of history with shared features.
A line that shows events in the order they happened.
An area of the world with shared features.
A challenge or problem that people have faced across many times and places.
Before the Common Era / Common Era — the way years are counted.
The study of Earth's land, water, climate, and people.
The usual weather of a place over a long time.
Low land along a river — where the first civilizations began.
Good for growing crops (rich soil).
Moving water to fields to grow crops.
The Old Stone Age, when people hunted and gathered food.
The change from hunting and gathering to farming and settled life.
Farming — growing crops and raising animals.
Taming plants and animals for human use.
A person who moves from place to place instead of settling.
A person who gets food by hunting animals and collecting plants.
Extra food or goods beyond what is needed.
Doing one special job, like potter or scribe, instead of farming.
An advanced society with cities, government, religion, jobs, and writing.
A city that rules itself and the land around it, like Athens or Ur.
Many lands and peoples ruled by one government.
The spread of ideas, goods, and customs from one culture to another.
An object made by people long ago, like a tool or pot.
A scientist who studies objects from the past.
The land between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers; home of the first cities.
The wedge-shaped writing of Mesopotamia — the world's first writing.
A large stepped temple in a Mesopotamian city.
The first major written law code, from Babylon.
A written set of laws for everyone to follow.
A person trained to write and keep records.
The god-king ruler of ancient Egypt.
Egyptian picture writing.
Paper-like material made from a river plant in Egypt.
A series of rulers from the same family.
The Chinese belief that heaven gives good rulers the right to rule.
Respect for parents and elders — a key Confucian value.
A large landmass that is part of a continent, like India.
Seasonal winds in Asia that bring heavy rain or dry weather.
A religion or philosophy — a set of ideas about how to live.
Belief in many gods.
Belief in one God.
Belief that spirits live in nature — animals, trees, rivers.
The major religion of India; includes karma, dharma, and reincarnation.
The strict social class system of India, set at birth.
In Hinduism, the duties a person must follow in life.
The idea that good or bad actions affect your next life.
The rebirth of the soul into a new body.
Religion begun by the Buddha; teaches the way to end suffering.
Buddhism's main teachings about suffering and how to end it.
Buddhism's eight steps of right living to end suffering.
In Buddhism, the state of perfect peace — the end of suffering and rebirth.
The religion of the Jewish people; the first major monotheistic faith.
The holy book of Judaism.
A sacred agreement, such as between God and the Jewish people.
The scattering of a people far from their homeland.
Religion based on the teachings of Jesus.
The moral laws of Judaism and Christianity.
Religion of Muslims, begun by the prophet Muhammad in Arabia.
The holy book of Islam.
The five duties every Muslim follows — faith, prayer, charity, fasting, pilgrimage.
A Muslim house of worship.
A person believed to deliver messages from God.
A religious journey to a holy place.
The Muslim pilgrimage to Mecca.
Chinese philosophy of Confucius: respect, order, and right relationships.
Chinese philosophy of living in harmony with nature (the Dao).
The Chinese idea of two opposite forces that balance each other.
A monotheistic religion that began in South Asia, blending new teachings with older traditions.
A person who travels to spread a religion.
A place where monks live, pray, and study.
Honoring family members who have died.
Ideas about right and wrong behavior.
A great early civilization whose ideas still influence us — Greece, Rome, Han China, Gupta India.
Government by the people.
Citizens vote on laws themselves, as in ancient Athens.
A member of a state with rights and duties.
Rule by a few powerful people.
The study of big questions using reason — truth, justice, how to live.
The blend of Greek, Egyptian, Persian, and Indian cultures after Alexander the Great.
Government where citizens elect leaders to represent them, as in Rome.
A member of Rome's wealthy, landowning upper class.
A common person in ancient Rome.
Rome's most powerful council of leaders.
Rome's first written laws, posted for all to see.
Two hundred years of peace and trade across the Roman Empire.
A Roman channel that carried water to cities.
China's test for choosing government officials by ability, not birth.
Chinese philosophy of strict laws and harsh punishments.
The long wall built to protect China's northern border.
A time of peace, wealth, and great achievements in art and science.
Connected routes where people exchange goods and ideas.
Overland trade routes linking China to the Middle East and Europe.
Sea trade connecting East Africa, Arabia, India, and China.
Camel caravan trade across the Sahara, especially gold and salt.
West Africa traded gold for Saharan salt — both highly valuable.
A person who buys and sells goods.
A group of traders traveling together, often with camels.
A desert place with water, where travelers rest.
Mali's richest king; his hajj to Mecca showed West Africa's gold wealth.
Mali's famous center of trade and Islamic learning.
A West African storyteller who keeps history alive by memory.
East African trading cities where African and Arab cultures blended.
Finding the way across the sea.
A Chinese invention that shows direction; helped sailors travel far.
A tool that uses the stars to find a ship's position.
A triangle-shaped sail that lets ships sail against the wind.
A city on the coast where ships load and unload goods.
Expensive items like silk, spices, and porcelain.
Fine Chinese pottery, traded around the world.
Flavorings like pepper and cinnamon — a major reason for trade.
Trading goods directly without money.
Money used for buying and selling.
Central Asian horsemen who built history's largest land empire.
A Mongol ruler, like Genghis Khan.
Peace across Mongol lands that made Silk Road trade safe.
Payment a weaker state gives a stronger one.
The disease that spread along trade routes and killed millions in the 1300s.
A disease that spreads quickly to many people.
Ming China's admiral who led huge treasure-fleet voyages across the Indian Ocean.
Muslim traveler who wrote about the lands of Africa and Asia.
Venetian traveler whose book described the riches of Yuan China.
The period in Europe between the fall of Rome and the Renaissance.
The eastern Roman Empire, centered at Constantinople.
The organized collection of Roman laws made by Emperor Justinian.
The Christian church of the Byzantine Empire and Russia.
The 1054 split of Christianity into Catholic (west) and Orthodox (east).
A holy image of Jesus or a saint used in worship.
The alphabet created for Slavic languages like Russian.
The emperor of Russia.
A system where lords give land in exchange for loyalty and military service.
A lord's self-sufficient estate where serfs farmed the land.
A noble who owned land and protected the people on it.
A noble who received land from a lord and promised loyalty.
A warrior on horseback who served a lord.
A peasant tied to the lord's land, not free to leave.
The knight's code of honor: bravery, loyalty, and protecting the weak.
A group of craftspeople or merchants who set rules for their trade.
Wars by European Christians to take the Holy Land; they increased trade with Asia.
The region around Jerusalem, sacred to Jews, Christians, and Muslims.
The head of the Roman Catholic Church.
Being expelled from the Church — a feared punishment in the Middle Ages.
The 1215 English document that limited the king's power.
A lawmaking body of representatives, first developed in England.
A Muslim ruler seen as the successor of Muhammad.
An Islamic empire ruled by a caliph.
A Muslim king, as in the Ottoman Empire.
A time when Muslim scholars led the world in math, science, and medicine.
The branch of math developed by Muslim scholars.
The art of beautiful writing, important in Islamic and Chinese culture.
A Japanese warrior who served a lord, like a European knight.
Japan's top military ruler — the real power, above the emperor.
The samurai code of honor, loyalty, and bravery.
A powerful Japanese landowning lord who commanded samurai.
A Chinese invention that spread west and changed warfare.
Printing with reusable characters — invented in Song China.
The Turkish Muslim empire that conquered Constantinople in 1453.
An elite soldier of the Ottoman sultan.
The Chinese dynasty (1368-1644) known for the Forbidden City and Zheng He's voyages.
The Ming emperor's vast palace in Beijing.
A policy of avoiding contact with other countries.
China's system where other states sent gifts to honor the emperor.
Passing history and stories by speaking, not writing.
Family ties that organize a community.
A powerful stone-walled trading city in southern Africa.
The region of Mexico and Central America where the Maya and Aztec lived.
Mesoamerican civilization known for pyramids, writing, and the calendar.
The empire of central Mexico with its island capital Tenochtitlan.
The Aztec capital, built on an island in a lake.
Aztec floating gardens used to grow food on the lake.
The Andes mountain empire of South America, connected by roads.
Cutting step-like fields into mountainsides to farm, as the Inca did.
Knotted cords the Inca used to keep records without writing.
Corn — the key food crop of the Americas.
The rebirth of art and learning in Europe, starting in Italy.
A focus on human achievements and classical Greek and Roman learning.
Worldly, not religious.
A wealthy person who pays artists to create works, like the Medici.
A painting technique that makes flat pictures look deep and real.
Gutenberg's machine that made books cheap and spread ideas fast.
The movement begun by Martin Luther that split Western Christianity.
A Christian who separated from the Catholic Church during the Reformation.
A Church pardon for sin sold for money — Luther's main complaint.
Luther's 1517 list of complaints against the Church.
The Catholic Church's campaign to reform itself and answer Protestantism.
A belief that goes against official Church teaching.
Church courts that tried people accused of heresy.
A king or queen with total power over the state.
The claim that monarchs get their power from God.
Copying European ways, as Peter the Great did in Russia.
The new way of learning about nature through observation and experiment.
The idea that planets orbit the sun (Copernicus, Galileo).
The old idea that everything orbits the Earth.
Testing ideas with experiments and evidence.
The age of reason — thinkers applied logic to government and society.
Rights all people are born with: life, liberty, property.
The agreement between people and government — rulers must serve the people.
The 1400s-1600s, when Europeans sailed the world seeking trade routes.
A small, fast ship that made long ocean voyages possible.
The science of making maps.
To sail all the way around the world.
A Spanish conqueror of the Americas, like Cortes or Pizarro.
The European disease that killed millions of Native Americans.
The body's protection against a disease.
The movement of plants, animals, people, and diseases between the Americas and the rest of the world.
The Atlantic trade loop linking Europe, Africa, and the Americas.
The brutal ocean voyage that carried enslaved Africans to the Americas.
The forced shipment of millions of Africans to the Americas.
A large farm growing one cash crop, worked by enslaved laborers.
A crop grown to sell, like sugar or tobacco.
The Spanish system that forced Native Americans to work for colonists.
A large Spanish colonial estate or ranch.
A person of mixed European and Indigenous American ancestry.
The idea that colonies exist to make the home country rich — export more than you import.
A land ruled by a distant country.
Taking control of another land and settling it.
A business where investors share the cost and profit of voyages.
An economic system of private business and trade for profit.
The growth of banks, companies, and world trade in early modern Europe.
Rising prices — as when American silver flooded into Spain.
Complete control of a product or trade by one group.
A good sold to another country.
A good bought from another country.
Natural resources used to make products, like cotton or wood.
Finished products made from raw materials.
The difference between what a country exports and imports.
The shift from hand-made goods to machine production in factories.
A building where machines and workers make goods.
The growth of cities as people move there for work.
